transcript.transcript: Started constructing. They had this old hangar building. And the hangar was built out of ten foot by ten foot steel and glass panels. And My dad and I, I came back out of the Air Force in college and I joined my dad. We went to bid on the building, the hangar building, And we bought the building and used that building for our couple's grounds mill. Is that right? It's still standing, by the way. Oh, so that was the old hangar. Yeah. Oh, how interesting. Our store was made out of that hangar. Those glass panels are still there. Why did they have glass panels in a hangar? They had sliding doors and for some reason they were glass, I guess, so they could see in, I suppose. But then the tennis in the college, actually, after the Second World War, the local tennis was still right across the river from the college. So at the college, they only had two courts at the time. And we did have a four-man team at the time, at Boise Junior College. And Dr. Obey, Don Obey, who was the biology teacher, was our coach. And he never played tennis either, but he sure knew his book, Tennis. He knew it by the book. Great guy. Is there quite a bit of leadership now from Mr. Appleton as president of Micron? Appleton has contributed a lot of donations to build up facilities. There's the Appleton Tennis Center, a wonderful center, probably one of the better ones in the United States. And then Coach Greg Patton, who is a world-class coach, is here. He loves Boise, and he's a tremendous addition to Boise. I hope he never leaves. We're really excited about him being here and the facilities they have. And it's connected now with Julia Davis Hart. So it's got a lot of history tied in. That's interesting. Well, I am interested in if there's anything that you can find about how this club got going, or who gave the money. Yeah, it would just be really interesting to go back a generation from you. Right. And maybe we can find it through, there's probably ways to find it. Maybe the statesman. Yeah, I know that a real historian can probably find it. Dick Deasum was one of the editors. He was very active. So they probably got some stuff in France, then. Right. I think they have some nice history on the tennis club. There's a lot of art in the heart. Yeah, he's head of the whole history. Yeah. Idaho history. Yeah, he might have. He might have. Yeah. What color were the plates? Were they brown? It was kind of a reddish brown. Reddish, just like clay, huh? Yeah. It's a wonderful surface to play on, because it's much easier on the legs and the heart cord. You Probably wouldn't have had to have that knee replaced. I was just going to say, if I would have played all my tennis on clay, I probably would have had my new knees. Right. But I'm back playing, so that's okay. Good. And you play now at the racket club? At the racket club, right. So now you've been playing for 70 years then. Yeah, you're dating me, yeah. Yeah, fortunately. That's a lifetime sport? Fortunately, I was very fortunate to take that sport up because it is a lifetime sport. Meet a lot of great people. And there were restrooms in the area where they're? I don't remember. I remember Harvard Chandler said something about iffy restrooms. There was maybe a couple once around. Yeah, I don't know the history of that. Yeah. Yeah. Anyway, but then they built the pavilion over where the picnics are. Right. So you'd have your picnics there. Right. Yeah. Happy right Bring back a lot of nice memories Well, I really appreciate you sharing with us Thank you for having me. It's been a pleasure and I'll be waiting for the anniversary part Okay, put that on our calendar, honey, won't we? Yeah. OK. Thank you, Paula, for joining in with us. News from Weezer. Oh, yeah. If you want any Weezer history, she's got that. In fact, your grandmother was, tell her about your grandmother. She was head of the Boise, or the Idaho Business Women's, or something. She was head of, what was her name? Well, no, she wasn't head of it. She was honored by them. Sophie Reinhart, this is all from Weezy. This is from Weezy. You haven't had to listen to this in 100 years. Really? That's right. Yeah, maybe some of our grandkids. Do you know anything about, this has nothing to do with the courts, but do you know anything about the Colubria? Have you ever heard that name? It was a mining camp above Israel. And I know Arthur heart you know all about it. I know it was a town. Your family's from there? Well and then they came to Weiser And I know that when my grandmother married my grandfather, they were going to put a business in. I think that's how you say it. Galubria. Is it Galubria? Right. Now, they have a book in the history, remember? About it. Yes. About it. A little bit about it. But anyway, it's just I never was told anything about this place. But I just kind of found out about it. And She said that she wouldn't marry him unless he went into business in the big city in New York. Oh really? She didn't want to go to Miami. Right. And that was above Midvale. I guess Larry Craig must know about it. Yeah. I've always been curious about where it was. Yeah. Well. You know how when you're young, when you're really young, like 17, 18, you're not too interested. And now everybody's gone now. I know. And now you're. Now you're really interested. I know. Yeah. Good interview. Yeah. Is your family still in California? I have a few, but most of them are. That's a very small family. Spread out. Probably better. Should we end it now? Oh. For the tape bird. All right. Thank you for having me. Thank you, Tommy. Nice to meet you. You
